structure.title: EROSION CONTROL AND FIRESCAPING WITH CALIFORNIA NATIVE PLANTS (Central and Southern Coastal California Counties)

nodes.title: DROUGHT TOLERANT & FIRE RESISTANT SHRUBS
nodes.summary: This partial document provides a list of California native plants suitable for erosion control and firescaping in Central and Southern Coastal California Counties. It categorizes plants into drought-tolerant and fire-resistant shrubs, fire-resistant trees, and a special section for fire-resistant trees and shrubs found in riparian, irrigated, or wet areas, noting their higher moisture content contributes to their resistance. Each plant is listed with its common and scientific name.

nodes.title: FIRE RESISTANT TREES
nodes.summary: This partial document provides a list of California native plants suitable for erosion control and firescaping in Central and Southern Coastal California Counties. It categorizes plants into drought-tolerant and fire-resistant shrubs, fire-resistant trees, and a special section for fire-resistant trees and shrubs found in riparian, irrigated, or wet areas, noting that higher moisture content increases their fire resistance. Each plant is listed with its common and scientific name.

nodes.title: FIRE RESISTANT TREES & SHRUBS (riparian, irrigated or wet areas)
nodes.summary: This partial document provides a comprehensive list of California native plants suitable for erosion control and firescaping in Central and Southern Coastal California Counties. It categorizes plants by type (shrubs, trees, perennial forbs, grasses, and groundcovers) and their specific characteristics, such as drought tolerance, fire resistance, and suitability for riparian or wet areas. Each entry includes both the common and scientific name of the plant, offering practical guidance for selecting appropriate vegetation to reduce fire risk and manage erosion.

nodes.title: FIRE RESISTANT NATIVE PERENNIAL FORBS
nodes.summary: This partial document provides a categorized list of native plants, often including their scientific names. It begins with a general list of various trees and shrubs, then specifically highlights "FIRE RESISTANT NATIVE PERENNIAL FORBS" and "FIRE RESISTANT NATIVE GRASSES & GROUNDCOVERS." The grasses and groundcovers are further itemized. Some entries include additional notes regarding growing conditions or specific varieties.

nodes.title: GROUNDCOVERS:
nodes.summary: This partial document provides extensive lists of fire-resistant native plants, categorized into trees/shrubs, perennial forbs, grasses, and groundcovers, often including their scientific names. It offers crucial guidance on the characteristics that make plants fire-resistant and best practices for fire-safe landscaping, such as proper spacing and maintenance. The document also identifies specific invasive/non-native plants and certain native species that pose a high fire hazard, recommending they be avoided or kept at a significant distance from structures. Finally, it lists resources for further information and notes the expert origin and collaborative development of the plant list following major fires.

structure.summary: This partial document provides a list of California native plants suitable for erosion control and firescaping in Central and Southern Coastal California Counties. It categorizes plants into drought-tolerant and fire-resistant shrubs, fire-resistant trees, and a special section for fire-resistant trees and shrubs found in riparian, irrigated, or wet areas, noting their higher moisture content contributes to their resistance. Each plant is listed with its common and scientific name.

structure.title: INVASIVE AND/OR HIGH FIRE HAZARD SHRUBS AND TREES
structure.summary:
This partial document provides guidance on selecting and managing plants for fire resistance and erosion control, particularly in restoration projects and home landscapes. It outlines general characteristics of fire-resistant plants, such as low sap/resin content, minimal accumulation of dead material, and high moisture content in leaves. The document also emphasizes the importance of proper plant spacing to prevent fire spread.

Crucially, it lists specific invasive and/or high fire hazard non-native plants to avoid, as well as native plants that should be discouraged near homes due to their high oil content or tendency to produce flammable dead material, recommending a 100-foot setback for these. Finally, it offers resources for further information on plant choices and fire-resistant landscaping, and provides context on the list's origin and contributors.
